GPT-4V and Gemini are both multi-modal models. You can show AI a photo and have it analyze the content, or give it an audio clip for transcription or analysis.
On-Device: AI models run on local devices (phones, computers) rather than in the cloud. This brings three major benefits: privacy protection (data stays on device), low latency (no network transmission needed), and offline availability.
Analogy#Multi-Modal: AI goes from “only hearing” to “hearing, seeing, and speaking” On-Device: AI goes from “living in the cloud” to “living in your phone” Representative Products#Product Features GPT-4V / Gemini Multi-modal understanding, supports image-text mixed input Apple Intelligence On-device AI, privacy-first Xiaomi, Huawei Phone AI Locally running intelligent assistants Future Trends#Multi-modal is becoming standard, and on-device AI is rapidly developing as chip performance improves. Future AI assistants will “live” in your devices, responding anytime while protecting privacy.
Team: Multi-Agent#Professional Division of Labor, Collaborative Completion#A single Agent has limited capabilities. Multi-Agent systems enable multiple AI “experts” to collaborate on complex tasks.
Imagine a software development team: product manager, frontend engineer, backend engineer, and QA engineer. Each role focuses on their domain while collaborating to complete the project.
Multi-Agent systems are similar: one Agent plans, one executes, one reviews, and one tests. They work together to complete complex tasks that a single Agent cannot handle.
Analogy#Single Agent: One person handles all the work Multi-Agent: A team divides and collaborates Representative Products#Product Function MetaGPT Multi-Agent software development team, capable of completing the full process from requirements to code AutoGen Open-source multi-Agent framework from Microsoft CrewAI Simplifies multi-Agent system construction Future Trends#Multi-Agent is a key direction for handling complex tasks. More “AI teams” will emerge in the future, each optimized for specific domains.
Foundation: AI Infra#The Cornerstone Supporting Everything#AI Infra (AI Infrastructure) is the underlying technology supporting AI operations, including:
Compute: GPUs, TPUs, NPUs, and other specialized chips Frameworks: PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX, and other training and inference frameworks Cloud Services: AWS, Azure, Alibaba Cloud, and other AI cloud platforms Inference Optimization: Model compression, quantization, distillation, and other techniques to make models run faster and more efficiently Analogy#If AI applications are cars, AI Infra is the roads, gas stations, and traffic systems. Without good infrastructure, even the best cars can’t run.
Representative Products/Technologies#Category Representatives Chips NVIDIA H100, AMD MI300, Huawei Ascend Frameworks PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX Cloud Platforms AWS Bedrock, Azure AI, Alibaba Cloud PAI Inference Optimization vLLM, TensorRT, ONNX Runtime Future Trends#AI Infra is developing toward “more efficient, cheaper, and easier to use.” Specialized chip performance continues to improve, inference costs keep dropping, making AI capabilities more accessible.
Summary#Capability Technology Core Value Brain LLM + Reasoning Understanding and reasoning, from fast thinking to slow thinking Memory Long Context + RAG Remembering information, short-term memory + long-term knowledge base Hands Agent + Tool Learning Executing tasks, from chatting to doing Nerves MCP Connecting tools, AI’s universal interface Body Multi-Modal + On-Device Perceiving the world, multi-modal + localization Team Multi-Agent Collaborative division of labor, handling complex tasks Foundation AI Infra Supporting operations, compute + frameworks + cloud services These seven capabilities work together, enabling AI to evolve from “chatbot” to true “digital assistant.” In 2026, we stand at the eve of an AI capability explosion.
Further Reading#OpenAI o1 Technical Report Anthropic: Claude’s Long Context Capabilities MCP Official Documentation LangChain: Agent Development Guide
title: AI Technology Landscape 2026: Seven Core Capabilities Explained

content:
Why Understanding Printers Matters#In the digital age, printers may seem “traditional,” but they remain indispensable devices in both home and office settings. Children’s homework needs printing, contracts require signatures, photos deserve physical preservation—these needs keep printers relevant.
However, the printer market is complex with numerous categories and specifications, plus the “affordable to buy, expensive to use” consumables trap, leaving many confused when making a purchase. This article will comprehensively analyze various printer types from working principles, suitable scenarios, and consumable costs to help you find the perfect match.
Printer Classification Overview#By working principle, mainstream printers can be categorized as follows:
Type Core Technology Primary Use Price Range Inkjet Printer Liquid ink ejection Home documents, photos $30-400 Laser Printer Laser imaging + toner fusing Business documents, bulk printing $70-700 Dot Matrix Printer Ribbon impact printing Receipts, multi-part forms $70-400 Thermal Printer Thermal paper coloration Receipts, labels $15-150 Dye-Sublimation Printer Thermal transfer dye Photo printing $70-400 Let’s examine each type in detail.
Inkjet Printers: Mainstream Choice for Home Users#Inkjet printers form images by ejecting liquid ink onto paper through print heads, making them the most common printer type for home users.
Working Principle#The core of inkjet printing is the print head, which contains hundreds or thousands of tiny nozzles. Based on the ejection method, there are two technical approaches:
Thermal Inkjet: Represented by HP and Canon. A heating element inside the nozzle instantly heats the ink to create a bubble, which expands and “squeezes” the ink droplet out. This method has a simple structure and low cost, but the print head has a relatively shorter lifespan.
Piezoelectric Inkjet: Represented by Epson. A piezoelectric crystal near the nozzle deforms when electrified, squeezing the ink out. This method offers longer print head life and more precise droplet control, but at a higher cost.
Key Parameters Explained#Resolution (DPI): Indicates the number of dots that can be printed per inch—higher values mean sharper output. 600dpi is sufficient for document printing, while 1200dpi+ is recommended for photos.
Print Speed (PPM): Pages per minute. Home inkjets typically print 10-20ppm (black and white), while photo printing is much slower.
Ink Color Count: Four colors (CMYK) is the basic configuration; six or more colors are suitable for photo printing, offering richer color gradation.
Print Head Type: Divided into integrated (replacing cartridge replaces head) and independent (only replace cartridge when empty). The former is easier to maintain but consumables are expensive; the latter has cheaper consumables but higher repair costs if the head clogs.
Ink Type Comparison#Ink Type Characteristics Suitable Scenarios Dye Ink Vivid colors, low cost, but not waterproof or fade-resistant Regular documents, daily photos Pigment Ink Waterproof, fade-resistant, long-lasting, but slightly duller colors Archive documents, professional photos Dye-Sublimation Ink Natural color transitions, waterproof, fade-resistant ID photos, artistic photos Pros and Cons Analysis#Pros:
Low machine cost, entry-level models available for $30-70 Excellent color printing, suitable for photos and color documents Compact size, suitable for home use Low power consumption, no ozone emission Cons:
Ink prone to drying, long idle periods can clog print heads Higher per-page printing cost (with original ink) Relatively slow printing speed Prints on plain paper can smudge when wet Suitable Scenarios#Home daily document printing Student homework and study materials Photo printing (choose photo printers) Low-frequency printing needs (power on at least once a week) Buying Recommendations#Ink Tank vs Cartridge: Ink tank models (refillable) have lower long-term costs; cartridge models are better for users with minimal printing needs.
Print Head Maintenance: Choose models with automatic cleaning and anti-clogging features to reduce maintenance hassles.
Connectivity: Support for WiFi, mobile direct printing, and WeChat printing makes usage more convenient.
Brand Support: Epson, Canon, and HP are the three mainstream brands with extensive service networks.
Laser Printers: Top Choice for Business#Laser printers use laser beams to create electrostatic latent images on photosensitive drums, then attract toner for transfer to paper, finally heat-fusing it. This technology originated from photocopiers and is known for speed and stable quality.
Working Principle#The laser printing process can be summarized in six steps: Charging → Exposure → Development → Transfer → Fusing → Cleaning.
Charging: The photosensitive drum surface is uniformly charged with negative electricity Exposure: Laser beam scans the drum, charge disappears in irradiated areas Development: Charged toner is attracted to areas where charge disappeared Transfer: Toner transfers from drum to paper Fusing: Heat and pressure melt the toner onto the paper Cleaning: Residual toner is cleared from the drum, ready for the next cycle Key Parameters Explained#Print Speed (PPM): Laser printers have a clear speed advantage. Entry-level: 20-30ppm, mid-range: 30-40ppm, high-end: 60ppm+.
Resolution: Mainstream 600×600dpi or 1200×1200dpi, completely sufficient for document printing.
First Page Out Time: Time from sending command to first page printed, affecting small-batch printing efficiency. Excellent models can achieve under 8 seconds.
Monthly Duty Cycle: Maximum pages the printer can handle per month. 5,000 pages/month is sufficient for home use; 10,000+ pages/month recommended for business.
Memory and Processor: Affects large file printing speed and network printing response. Business models typically have 128MB+ memory.
Monochrome vs Color Laser#Comparison Monochrome Laser Color Laser Machine Price $70-280 $200-700 Per-Page Cost $0.004-0.01 $0.02-0.05 Print Speed Faster Slightly slower Maintenance Complexity Simple More complex (four toner cartridges) Suitable Scenarios Document-focused Need color documents Pros and Cons Analysis#Pros:
Fast printing speed, suitable for bulk printing Sharp, clear text, ideal for business documents No clogging, works after long idle periods Low per-page printing cost (monochrome) Waterproof, fade-resistant, long-lasting Cons:
Higher machine cost Color printing quality inferior to inkjet Higher power consumption, minor ozone emission Larger footprint Cannot print photos Suitable Scenarios#Business office, corporate documents High-frequency printing needs Long idle periods, occasional printing Need for rapid bulk printing Buying Recommendations#Consumables Cost: Laser printer consumables are toner cartridges/drum units, with significant brand differences. Research consumable prices and page yields before purchasing.
Drum-Toner Separation: Integrated drum-toner is simpler to replace but costs more; separated drum-toner is more economical but slightly more complex to operate.
Feature Requirements: Consider whether you need automatic duplexing, automatic document feeder (ADF), network printing, etc.
Brand Reputation: HP, Canon, Brother, and Fuji Xerox are mainstream brands, each with their strengths.
Dot Matrix Printers: Specialized for Receipts#Dot matrix printers use pins on the print head to strike a ribbon, leaving ink marks on paper. Although the technology is old, it remains irreplaceable in specific fields.
Working Principle#Multiple steel pins (commonly 9 or 24 pins) are arranged on the print head, driven by electromagnets to strike the ribbon, creating characters or images formed by dot matrix on paper.
Key Parameters Explained#Pin Count: 9-pin for simple receipts, 24-pin for finer print quality.
Print Width: 80-column (A4 width) and 136-column (A3 width) are common specifications.
Print Speed: Measured in characters per second (CPS), typically 200-400 CPS.
Copy Capability: How many layers of carbon copies can be printed, commonly 1+3 (original + 3 copies) or 1+6.
Print Head Life: Measured in hundreds of millions of strikes, typically 200-400 million.
Pros and Cons Analysis#Pros:
Can print multi-part carbon copies—irreplaceable by other printers Cheap consumables (ribbon) Simple structure, durable and reliable Suitable for continuous paper printing Cons:
Slow printing speed Rough print quality Noisy operation Can only print dot matrix graphics, not suitable for photos Suitable Scenarios#Invoice and receipt printing Multi-part form printing Banking, taxation, logistics industries Passbook printing Buying Recommendations#Purpose: Choose flatbed type for invoices, roller type for continuous paper.
Copy Capability: Select based on business needs for how many copies are required.
Brand: Epson LQ series, Jolimark, and Dascom are mainstream brands.
Thermal Printers: Receipt and Label Specialists#Thermal printers work on the principle that thermal paper changes color when heated, requiring no ink or ribbon. Commonly found in cashier receipts, shipping labels, etc.
Working Principle#Thermal paper has a special chemical coating on its surface that changes color through chemical reaction when heated. The printer heats specific positions on the thermal paper through a thermal head to form text or patterns.
Key Parameters Explained#Paper Width: 58mm is standard for receipt printers, 80mm for wider receipts, label printers commonly 4 inches (about 100mm).
Print Speed: Measured in mm/s, commonly 50-150 mm/s.
Resolution: 203dpi is mainstream, high-end models offer 300dpi.
Print Length: Maximum single print length, affecting long label printing.
Pros and Cons Analysis#Pros:
No ink or ribbon needed, single consumable Simple structure, easy maintenance Fast printing speed Compact size, portable Quiet operation Cons:
Short thermal paper shelf life (typically 2-5 years) Not heat or light resistant, text will fade Can only print single color Relatively higher paper cost Suitable Scenarios#Cashier receipt printing Shipping labels, label printing Portable printing scenarios Temporary voucher printing Buying Recommendations#Purpose: Choose 58mm portable model for receipts, 4-inch desktop model for labels.
Connectivity: Choose USB, Bluetooth, or WiFi based on usage scenario.
Brand: Xprinter, Gainscha, and Deli are representative domestic brands; Zebra is an international brand.
Dye-Sublimation Printers: Optimal for Photos#Dye-sublimation printers use solid dye that transfers to specialized photo paper through heating, producing images close to photo development quality.
Working Principle#Solid dye on the ribbon sublimates (directly changes from solid to gas) under the thermal head’s action, penetrating the photo paper coating. Each color is printed separately, typically yellow, magenta, and cyan, with some models adding a transparent protective layer.
Key Parameters Explained#Resolution: Typically 300×300dpi, but due to the dye penetration characteristic, actual quality far exceeds inkjet at the same resolution.
Ribbon Specification: Labeled by photo size and quantity, such as 4×6 inches 108 sheets.
Photo Size: Common sizes include 4×6 inches (6-inch), 5×7 inches (7-inch), 6×8 inches.
Print Time: One 6-inch photo typically takes 1-2 minutes.
Pros and Cons Analysis#Pros:
Excellent photo printing quality with natural color transitions Waterproof and fingerprint-resistant, long-lasting No print head clogging issues Simple operation, one-touch printing Cons:
Can only print photos, not documents Paper and ribbon must be used as matched sets Higher per-sheet cost (about $0.10-0.40/sheet) Fixed photo sizes Suitable Scenarios#Home photo printing ID photo printing Photography enthusiasts Creative product making Buying Recommendations#Photo Size: Choose supported sizes based on needs; 6-inch is most versatile.
Brand: Canon SELPHY series, Fujifilm, and Xiaomi all offer good products.
Portability: Portable and desktop models available; choose based on usage scenario.
Consumables Cost Deep Dive#The purchase cost of a printer is just the tip of the iceberg; the true long-term cost lies in consumables. Let’s analyze consumable costs for various printer types in detail.
Inkjet Printer Consumables#Cartridge Type: Integrated cartridge and print head, replacing cartridge replaces head.
Item Cost Black cartridge (~200 pages) $5-12 Color cartridge (~150 pages) $8-15 Per-page cost (B&W) $0.03-0.06 Per-page cost (Color) $0.05-0.10 Ink Tank Type: Large-capacity ink tanks, refillable.
Item Cost Black ink (~6000 pages) $8-15 Color ink (~6000 pages) $8-15 Per-page cost (B&W) $0.001-0.003 Per-page cost (Color) $0.003-0.006 Conclusion: For users with high print volumes, ink tank models have significant long-term cost advantages. At 100 pages per month, cartridge-type annual consumable cost is about $40-70, while ink tank type is only $3-6.
Laser Printer Consumables#Toner Cartridge/Drum: Core consumables for laser printers.
Item Cost Monochrome drum (~1500-3000 pages) $15-45 Color toner (~1000-2000 pages/color) $20-45/color Per-page cost (B&W) $0.004-0.01 Per-page cost (Color) $0.02-0.05 Drum-Toner Separation vs Integration:
Integrated: Replace drum unit, simple operation, but higher cost Separated: Replace only toner when empty, drum lasts longer, lower cost Conclusion: Monochrome laser printing cost is comparable to ink tank inkjet, but machine price is higher. Color laser cost is significantly higher than inkjet.
Dot Matrix Printer Consumables#Ribbon: The only consumable for dot matrix printers.
Item Cost Ribbon core (~3-5 million characters) $1-2 Ribbon frame (with core) $2-6 Conclusion: Dot matrix printer consumable costs are extremely low, but limited to specific uses.
Thermal Printer Consumables#Thermal Paper: The only consumable.
Item Cost 58mm thermal paper (~30m) $0.30-0.80 80mm thermal paper (~50m) $0.80-1.50 Label paper (~100 sheets) $1.50-5 Conclusion: Moderate consumable cost, but thermal paper has limited shelf life.
Dye-Sublimation Printer Consumables#Ribbon + Photo Paper Set: Must be used as matched sets.
Item Cost 6-inch set (~108 sheets) $12-22 Per-sheet cost $0.10-0.20 Conclusion: Higher per-sheet cost, but best photo quality.
Paper Types and Selection#Different printers use different types of paper. Choosing the right paper yields better print results.
Inkjet Paper#Paper Type Characteristics Suitable Scenarios Plain paper Low cost, suitable for daily documents Documents, drafts Inkjet paper Surface coating, no ink bleeding Important documents Glossy photo paper High gloss, vivid colors Photo printing Matte photo paper No reflection, premium texture Artistic photos RC photo paper Waterproof coating, long-lasting Professional photos Laser Paper#Laser printers have low paper requirements; regular copy paper works fine. But note:
Paper weight: 70g is standard, 80g is thicker, 100g suitable for duplex printing Paper brightness: Higher is whiter, but overly white may contain fluorescent agents Paper flatness: Damp or curled paper jams easily Dot Matrix Paper#Paper Type Characteristics Suitable Scenarios Continuous paper Perforated, continuous printing Batch receipts Single sheet Single-sheet feeding Occasional printing Multi-part carbon paper Pressure-sensitive copies Multi-part forms Thermal Paper#Paper Type Shelf Life Suitable Scenarios Standard thermal paper 1-2 years Temporary receipts Long-life thermal paper 5-10 years Important vouchers Three-proof thermal paper Water/oil/alcohol resistant Special environments Common Features Explained#Modern printers have increasingly rich features. Here’s an explanation of common functions.
Automatic Duplex Printing#Automatically completes double-sided printing without manual paper flipping. Suitable for users who frequently print double-sided documents, saving paper and time.
Automatic Document Feeder (ADF)#Automatically feeds multiple pages for copying or scanning without placing each page individually. Suitable for users who frequently copy or scan multi-page documents.
Network Printing#Connects to network via Ethernet or WiFi, allowing multiple computers to share one printer. Practical for both home and office.
Mobile Printing#Supports direct printing through mobile apps, WeChat mini-programs, AirPrint, etc. Very practical for home users.
Cloud Printing#Remote printing via internet, sending print jobs even when not near the printer. Suitable for mobile office scenarios.
Scanning and Copying#All-in-one machines integrate scanning and copying functions at good value. Recommended for home users—more cost-effective than buying separately.
Fax#Traditional fax function, now rarely used. Unless you have special needs, not essential.
Purchase Decision Guide#Based on different usage scenarios, here are purchase recommendations.
Home Users#Low print volume (under 50 pages/month):
Recommendation: Cartridge-type inkjet all-in-one Budget: $40-85 Brands: HP, Canon, Epson entry-level models Note: Power on at least once a week to prevent print head clogging Medium print volume (50-200 pages/month):
Recommendation: Ink tank inkjet all-in-one Budget: $85-210 Brands: Epson EcoTank series, Canon G series, HP Smart Tank series Note: Choose models with WiFi and mobile printing features Photo printing needs:
Recommendation: Ink tank inkjet (6+ colors) or dye-sublimation photo printer Budget: $140-280 Brands: Epson 6-color ink tank, Canon SELPHY Student Families#K-12 homework printing:
Recommendation: Ink tank inkjet all-in-one Budget: $85-140 Features: Support mobile printing, WeChat printing for easy student self-operation Note: Choose color printing, some assignments require it University thesis printing:
Recommendation: Monochrome laser printer Budget: $70-140 Features: Duplex printing, network printing Note: Thesis printing volume is high, laser is more economical Home Office / Small Business#Document-focused:
Recommendation: Monochrome laser all-in-one Budget: $140-280 Features: Auto duplex, ADF, network printing Brands: Brother, HP, Canon Need color:
Recommendation: Color laser all-in-one or ink tank inkjet Budget: $210-420 Features: Auto duplex, network printing Note: Color laser costs more; ink tank is more economical but slower Business Office#High-frequency printing:
Recommendation: Mid-to-high speed monochrome laser printer Budget: $280-700 Features: High-speed printing, large paper tray, network printing Brands: HP LaserJet Pro/Enterprise, Canon iR series Color business documents:
Recommendation: Color laser printer Budget: $420-1100 Features: Auto duplex, network printing, high monthly duty cycle Brands: Fuji Xerox, Canon, HP Special Needs#Invoice/receipt printing:
Recommendation: Dot matrix printer Budget: $70-210 Type: Flatbed (invoices) or roller (continuous paper) Brands: Epson LQ series, Jolimark Shipping labels/labels:
Recommendation: Thermal label printer Budget: $30-70 Specification: 4-inch (100mm) Brands: Xprinter, Gainscha, Zebra ID photos/artistic photos:
Recommendation: Dye-sublimation photo printer Budget: $70-210 Brands: Canon SELPHY, Fujifilm Usage and Maintenance Recommendations#Inkjet Printer Maintenance#Power on regularly: At least once a week, let the printer automatically clean the print head Proper shutdown: Use the power button to shut down, allowing the print head to return to protected position Avoid long power-off periods: After power loss, the print head is exposed to air and prone to drying Use original ink: Compatible ink may clog print heads or affect colors Regular cleaning: Clean printer interior and exterior to maintain good condition Laser Printer Maintenance#Regular cleaning: Clean printer interior, remove paper scraps and toner residue Ensure ventilation: Laser printers produce small amounts of ozone during operation; maintain ventilation Use quality paper: Low-quality paper produces more paper scraps, affecting printer life Replace consumables timely: Replace drum unit when life expires to avoid affecting print quality Avoid frequent power cycling: Laser printers have high power consumption during startup Dot Matrix Printer Maintenance#Regular cleaning: Clean print head and paper feed mechanism Replace ribbon timely: Replace ribbon when exhausted to avoid print head directly striking paper Adjust print head gap: Adjust based on paper thickness to avoid print head damage Note lubrication: Regularly lubricate the paper feed mechanism Thermal Printer Maintenance#Clean print head: Regularly clean with alcohol cotton Use quality thermal paper: Low-quality paper accelerates print head wear Avoid high-temperature environments: High temperatures cause thermal paper to change color Watch for moisture: Damp thermal paper affects print quality Common Problems and Solutions#Inkjet Printer Common Issues#Print head clogging:
Symptoms: Stripes or missing colors in prints Solution: Use printer’s built-in cleaning function; for severe cases, clean multiple times or soak the print head Low ink warning too early:
Symptoms: Warning shows low ink but can still print Solution: This is the manufacturer’s “ink reserve protection” mechanism; can ignore warning and continue printing Print color deviation:
Symptoms: Print colors don’t match screen display Solution: Calibrate monitor, use ICC profiles, select correct paper type Laser Printer Common Issues#Paper jam:
Symptoms: Paper stuck inside printer Solution: Remove jammed paper following manual instructions; check if paper is damp or curled Black spots or streaks on prints:
Symptoms: Regular black spots or streaks on printouts Solution: Clean or replace drum unit; check if photosensitive drum is damaged Faint printing:
Symptoms: Printouts appear light Solution: Check toner level, adjust print density settings, replace drum unit General Issues#Cannot connect to printer:
Check if connection cable is properly plugged in Check if network connection is normal Reinstall driver Restart printer and computer Slow printing:
Lower print quality settings Check if there are many print jobs queued Check network connection speed Add printer memory (if supported) Summary#Core Purchase Principles#Clarify needs: Print volume, content type, and frequency are the three core considerations Focus on consumable costs: Machine price is a one-time investment; consumables are the long-term cost Choose reliable brands: Mainstream brands have guaranteed support; avoid off-brand traps Features should be sufficient: No need to pursue full features; what fits you is best Quick Decision Table#Need Scenario Recommended Type Budget Reference Home low-frequency printing Cartridge inkjet $40-85 Home medium-frequency printing Ink tank inkjet $85-210 Home photo printing 6-color inkjet/dye-sublimation $140-280 Student homework printing Ink tank inkjet $85-140 Home office documents Monochrome laser all-in-one $140-280 Small business office Monochrome laser/color laser $210-700 Invoice/receipt printing Dot matrix printer $70-210 Shipping label printing Thermal label printer $30-70 Final Advice#Printers are durable goods; a good printer can last for years. Don’t overthink when purchasing—choose based on your actual needs. Remember: there’s no perfect printer, only the one most suitable for you.
Hope this article helps you make an informed choice and find the printer that’s perfect for you!
title: Printer Buying Guide: From Inkjet to Laser, Find the Perfect One for You

content:
Why Build Your Own PC?#In an era where pre-built PCs and laptops are everywhere, building your own computer might seem like unnecessary effort. However, DIY PC building still offers irreplaceable advantages:
Better Value for Money. With the same budget, a self-assembled PC often delivers better performance than branded machines. Pre-built PCs need to cover R&D, marketing, and after-sales costs, while DIY only pays for the hardware itself.
Fully Customizable. You can configure precisely according to personal needs—gamers can allocate more budget to the graphics card, content creators can prioritize CPU and memory, and storage enthusiasts can fill up drive bays. No more “one-size-fits-all” from branded machines.
Transparent and Controllable. Every component’s model and specifications are crystal clear. No need to worry about common “cut corners” in pre-built PCs—like overstated power supply ratings, budget motherboards, or inadequate cooling.
Fun and Growth. Assembling a computer with your own hands, understanding the role of each component—this sense of achievement and accumulated knowledge cannot be obtained by simply buying one. When minor issues arise, you’ll be better equipped to troubleshoot and solve them yourself.
Of course, DIY PC building has its barriers: you need to learn hardware knowledge, spend time comparing prices, and do the assembly yourself. The goal of this article is to help you overcome these barriers.
Core Components Explained#A computer consists of multiple hardware components working together, each with its specific role. Understanding the function and selection criteria of these components is the foundation of PC configuration.
CPU: The Brain of the Computer#CPU (Central Processing Unit) is the core of the computer, responsible for executing various calculations and instructions. Think of it as the “commander-in-chief” of a factory, determining the processing speed and efficiency of the entire computer.
Key Parameters Explained#Core Count and Thread Count. The number of cores determines how many independent tasks the CPU can process simultaneously, while thread count is the logical parallel processing capability. Modern CPUs generally support hyper-threading technology (Intel calls it Hyper-Threading, AMD calls it SMT), where one physical core can simulate two logical threads.
An analogy: core count is the number of workshops in a factory, thread count is the number of workers. 4 cores and 8 threads means 4 workshops with 8 workers, each workshop having 2 workers working in shifts.
For daily office work and light gaming, 4-6 cores are sufficient; for gaming with streaming or video editing, 8+ cores are recommended; for professional rendering, compiling, and other heavy tasks, consider 12-16 cores or more.
Base Clock and Boost Clock. The base clock is the CPU’s fundamental operating frequency, while boost clock is the maximum frequency automatically achieved under high load. Higher frequencies mean stronger single-core performance.
Note that CPUs from different generations and architectures cannot be compared purely by frequency. A 3.0GHz CPU with new architecture might be faster than a 4.0GHz CPU with old architecture—just like a new 2.0T engine might outperform an old 3.0L engine.
Cache. CPU cache is high-speed storage located inside the CPU, divided into L1, L2, and L3 levels. Larger cache means higher hit rate when the CPU accesses data, resulting in better performance. L3 cache has a significant impact on gaming performance.
Manufacturing Process. Smaller process numbers mean more densely packed transistors and better energy efficiency. Currently, 5nm and 7nm processes are mainstream. CPUs with advanced processes typically have lower heat and power consumption.
Intel vs AMD: How to Choose?#Both camps have their advantages:
Feature Intel AMD Single-core Performance Traditional advantage, great gaming Rapidly catching up, gap narrowing Multi-core Value Mid-to-high-end products pricier More cores at same price point Power Consumption Recent generations run hotter Clear energy efficiency advantage Platform Compatibility New sockets with new generations AM4/AM5 platforms last longer Best For Pursuing maximum gaming FPS Multi-tasking, value priority Selection advice: Gamers should prioritize single-core performance and cache; content creators should prioritize multi-core performance. Check the latest CPU hierarchy charts and choose based on budget and needs.
Selection Strategy#Define Your Purpose. Office and home use: entry-level (i3/R3 or i5/R5); Gaming: mainstream (i5/R5); Professional work: mid-to-high-end (i7/R7 or above).
Generation Over Positioning. i5-13600K beats i7-11700K because generational gaps often outweigh positioning differences. “Buy new, not old” is an important principle for CPU selection.
Pay Attention to Suffixes. Intel: K=unlocked, F=no integrated graphics, KF=unlocked without iGPU; AMD: X=enhanced, 3D=stacked cache (gaming optimized). Choose based on needs—don’t pay for features you won’t use.
Tray vs Boxed. Tray CPUs are cheaper but have no official warranty; boxed CPUs cost more but have complete warranty. Beginners should choose boxed; experienced builders can consider tray.
Graphics Card: The Core of Graphics Processing#GPU (Graphics Processing Unit) handles graphics rendering tasks and is one of the most watched components for gamers and content creators.
Key Parameters Explained#VRAM Capacity and Bus Width. VRAM is the graphics card’s “memory,” storing graphics data to be processed. VRAM capacity determines how complex scenes can be handled, while bus width determines data transfer speed.
4GB VRAM is suitable for 1080P light gaming, 8GB for 1080P/2K mainstream gaming, 12GB+ for 4K gaming or professional rendering. 128-bit bus width is entry-level, 192-256-bit is mainstream, 384-bit+ is high-end.
CUDA Cores / Stream Processors. These are the “computing units” of the graphics card—the more there are, the stronger the parallel computing capability. NVIDIA calls them CUDA cores, AMD calls them stream processors. Note that the two architectures differ, so numbers cannot be directly compared.
VRAM Type. GDDR6 is mainstream, GDDR6X is faster, HBM/HBM2 is for top-tier professional cards. Higher VRAM frequency is better.
Ray Tracing and DLSS/FSR. Ray tracing is a new generation rendering technology that presents more realistic lighting and shadow effects. DLSS (NVIDIA) and FSR (AMD) are upscaling technologies that can significantly boost frame rates with minimal quality loss.
NVIDIA vs AMD: How to Choose?#Feature NVIDIA AMD Ray Tracing Performance Leading, mature hardware acceleration Supported but significant performance loss Upscaling DLSS has better results FSR is open and universal Productivity CUDA ecosystem complete, strong AI acceleration Suitable for pure gaming scenarios Price Higher at same tier Better value Driver Stability Mature and stable Significant improvement in recent years Selection advice: For ray tracing and AI features, choose NVIDIA; for pure gaming value, choose AMD; for professional productivity, prioritize NVIDIA (CUDA ecosystem).
Pitfall Avoidance Guide#Beware of “Mining Cards”. After the cryptocurrency boom, many used graphics cards flooded the market. Mining cards ran at high load for long periods and have questionable lifespan. Beginners should buy new; buying used requires discrimination skills.
Look at Model, Not Just VRAM. GT 1030 4GB is worse than GTX 1650 4GB because the core performance gap is huge. VRAM is not the only indicator—core performance matters more.
Note Power Supply Requirements. High-end graphics cards consume a lot of power—RTX 4090 even needs 450W+. When selecting a graphics card, consider power supply capacity simultaneously.
Brand Differences. The same chip from different brands has different build quality, cooling, and warranty. Top-tier brands (ASUS, MSI, Gigabyte, etc.) have more reliable quality; second and third-tier brands offer better value.
Memory: The Temporary Workspace for Data#RAM (Random Access Memory) is the bridge between CPU and storage, holding data and programs currently in use. More memory means more programs can run simultaneously; faster memory means higher data read/write efficiency.
Key Parameters Explained#Capacity. 8GB is the minimum threshold, barely enough for daily office work; 16GB is the mainstream recommendation, handling gaming and light creation; 32GB is suitable for heavy multi-tasking and professional creation; 64GB+ is for professional workstations.
Frequency. Memory frequency is measured in MHz—higher frequency means faster speed. DDR4 mainstream frequency is 2666-3600MHz, DDR5 mainstream is 4800-6400MHz. However, frequency improvements have diminishing returns, so don’t blindly pursue high frequencies.
Timing. Timing represents the latency of memory accessing data, commonly formatted as CL16-18-18-38. Lower timing is better at the same frequency, with CL being the most important indicator. CL16-18 is excellent for DDR4, CL30-40 is mainstream for DDR5.
Generation. DDR4 and DDR5 are currently mainstream. DDR5 has higher frequency and lower power consumption but also higher price. When choosing, note which generation your motherboard supports—they are not compatible.
The Importance of Dual Channel#Dual channel means using two memory sticks, allowing the memory controller to read and write data through two channels simultaneously, theoretically doubling bandwidth.
An analogy: single channel is one lane, dual channel is two lanes—more vehicles can pass in the same time.
For users with integrated graphics, dual channel is especially important—integrated graphics use system memory as VRAM, and dual channel significantly improves graphics performance. For discrete graphics users, dual channel also provides 10-20% improvement in gaming frame rates.
Practical advice: For 16GB memory, choose 2×8GB instead of 1×16GB; for 32GB, choose 2×16GB or 4×8GB.
Selection Strategy#Capacity Before Frequency. Insufficient capacity is more painful than low frequency—prioritize capacity.
Match Frequency to CPU. CPUs have upper limits on supported memory frequency; exceeding the limit will downclock. For example, if CPU supports DDR4 3200, buying DDR4 3600 will downclock to 3200.
Brand Selection. Top-tier brands like Kingston, G.Skill, ADATA, and Corsair offer stable quality; domestic brands like Gloway and Asgard offer good value. Regular users don’t need to pursue “gaming heatsinks”—practicality is key.
Note Compatibility. Some motherboards have limited support for high-frequency memory; check the motherboard’s memory compatibility list (QVL) before purchasing.
Storage: The Permanent Warehouse for Data#Storage is where the operating system, software, and files are kept. Unlike memory, data in storage doesn’t disappear when power is off.
SSD vs HDD#SSD (Solid State Drive) uses flash memory chips to store data—extremely fast, silent, and shock-resistant. It’s the current mainstream choice.
HDD (Hard Disk Drive) uses magnetic platters to store data—slow, noisy, and sensitive to vibration, but cheap with large capacity and relatively easy data recovery.
Current build configurations typically use SSD as the system drive and frequently used software drive, with HDD as large-capacity storage (if needed).
NVMe vs SATA#These are two interface types for SSDs.
NVMe SSD uses the M.2 interface and transfers data through PCIe channels—extremely fast. PCIe 3.0 x4 sequential read speeds can reach 3500MB/s, PCIe 4.0 can reach 7000MB/s, and PCIe 5.0 breaks 10000MB/s.
SATA SSD uses the SATA interface with a speed limit of about 550MB/s, but has good compatibility and low price.
Selection advice: System drive and frequently used software drive should be NVMe SSD; large-capacity storage can be SATA SSD or HDD.
Capacity Planning#256GB: Only for OS and a few programs, not recommended 512GB: Entry-level choice, OS + common software + a few games 1TB: Mainstream recommendation, OS + software + games + files, ample space 2TB+: Ideal for gamers and creators, no need to worry about space Selection Strategy#Look at Controller and NAND. The controller is the SSD’s “CPU,” affecting performance and lifespan; NAND determines capacity and durability. Original NAND (Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron, WD/SanDisk, Kioxia) has the best quality.
Pay Attention to TBW. TBW (Terabytes Written) indicates the total amount of data that can be written during the warranty period—higher numbers mean longer lifespan. Mainstream 1TB SSDs have TBW around 600.
Performance Tiers. Entry-level (no cache) suitable for regular users; mainstream (with cache) suitable for gaming and creation; flagship (high-end controller + cache) suitable for enthusiasts.
Brand Recommendations. Top-tier: Samsung, WD, SK Hynix, Kioxia; Value: ZHITAI, Gloway, Kingston.
Secondary Components Overview#Motherboard: The Hub Connecting Everything#The motherboard is the carrier for all hardware, responsible for connecting and coordinating all components.
Chipset determines the motherboard’s expansion capabilities and features. Intel mainstream chipsets: H610 (entry), B760 (mainstream), Z790 (high-end); AMD mainstream chipsets: A620 (entry), B650 (mainstream), X670 (high-end).
Power Phases affect CPU stability and overclocking potential. High-end CPUs need more power phases; entry motherboards might not handle them.
Expansion Interfaces include M.2 slot count, SATA port count, PCIe slots, etc. Choose based on future upgrade needs.
Selection advice: Motherboard must match CPU socket, power delivery must meet CPU needs, expansion interfaces should consider future upgrades. No need to pursue top-tier motherboards—sufficient is best.
Power Supply: The Energy Source for the System#The power supply provides electricity to all components, and its quality directly affects system stability.
Power Calculation. Estimate total power based on CPU and graphics card consumption, with 30% headroom recommended. For example: CPU 65W + GPU 200W + Others 50W = 315W, recommend 450W+ power supply.
Certification Level. 80 Plus certification indicates conversion efficiency, from low to high: White, Bronze, Silver, Gold, Platinum, Titanium. Gold and above offer the best value.
Modularity Type. Full modular (all cables detachable) for clean cable management; semi-modular (some cables detachable) for value; non-modular (fixed cables) for lowest price.
Selection advice: Leave headroom for power, choose Gold certification or above, select top-tier brands (Seasonic, Super Flower, FSP, etc.) or reliable second-tier. Power supply is a common area to cut costs, but it’s the last place you should.
Case: The Home for Hardware#The case houses all hardware and affects cooling, noise, and aesthetics.
Airflow Design. Mainstream is front-in rear-out, bottom-in top-out airflow. More fan mounting positions mean greater cooling potential.
Compatibility. Case must fit motherboard size (ATX/mATX/ITX), graphics card length, cooler height, and power supply length.
Cable Management Space. Adequate space behind the motherboard tray makes cable management easier, benefiting cooling and aesthetics.
Selection advice: Determine motherboard size and graphics card length first, then choose case. Pay attention to airflow design and build quality—no need to pursue flashy appearances.
CPU Cooler: The Guardian of CPU Temperature#CPUs generate significant heat during operation and need coolers to dissipate it.
Air Coolers dissipate heat through metal fins and fans—simple structure, high reliability. Entry-level (under $15) suitable for low-power CPUs; mainstream ($15-40) for mid-range CPUs; high-end dual-tower ($40+) can handle high-end CPUs.
All-in-One Liquid Coolers dissipate heat through pump, radiator, and coolant circulation—higher cooling efficiency but with minimal leak risk. 120mm AIO underperforms similarly priced air coolers; 240mm suits mid-to-high-end CPUs; 360mm suits top-tier CPUs.
Selection advice: Regular users should choose air cooling—better value and reliability; those pursuing extreme overclocking or preferring AIO aesthetics can choose liquid cooling. Note that cooler height must be compatible with the case.
Assembly Process and Tips#Once all hardware arrives, assembly can begin. Here’s the basic process:
Preparation#Prepare Tools: Phillips screwdriver (magnetic tip preferred), zip ties, work surface Read the Manual: Motherboard manual is most important—understand port locations and jumper definitions Anti-static: Touch metal objects to discharge static, or wear anti-static wrist strap Assembly Steps#Step 1: Install CPU
Open the CPU socket retention lever on the motherboard, confirm CPU and socket alignment notches match, gently place the CPU, close the retention lever. Don’t force it—the CPU should drop in naturally.
Step 2: Install Memory
Confirm memory slot locations; for dual channel, use slots 2 and 4. Open memory clips, align with notches, press down evenly until you hear a “click” indicating proper installation.
Step 3: Install M.2 SSD
Find the M.2 slot on the motherboard, insert the SSD, tighten the mounting screw. Some motherboards have heatsinks for M.2 slots—remember to install them.
Step 4: Install CPU Cooler
Apply thermal paste (if cooler doesn’t have pre-applied paste), install cooler mounting bracket, secure the cooler, connect fan power cable to motherboard CPU_FAN header.
Step 5: Install Motherboard in Case
Install motherboard standoffs in the case (positions matching motherboard holes), place motherboard in case, align with standoff holes, tighten screws. Don’t fully tighten at once—pre-tighten then gradually tighten all.
Step 6: Install Power Supply
Place power supply in the PSU bay, fan orientation depends on case design, tighten mounting screws.
Step 7: Install Graphics Card
Remove corresponding case slot covers, insert graphics card into motherboard PCIe slot until you hear the clip lock, tighten graphics card mounting screws. Connect graphics card power cables (if needed).
Step 8: Connect Cables
Motherboard power: 24pin large connector CPU power: 4pin or 8pin, usually at motherboard top-left Graphics card power: 6pin or 8pin, connect to graphics card Front panel: Power switch, reset, LEDs, USB, audio—connect according to motherboard manual Step 9: Cable Management
Use zip ties to organize cables neatly, avoid blocking airflow. Cases with good cable management space can hide cables behind the motherboard tray.
Step 10: Check and Power On
Check all cable connections are secure, confirm no issues, connect power, press power button. First boot may require entering BIOS to set boot order and XMP/EXPO memory overclocking.
Common Issues and Pitfall Avoidance#Beginner Mistakes#CPU Installed Backwards. CPU and socket have alignment features—if it doesn’t fit, don’t force it. Check orientation.
Memory Not Fully Seated. Improperly installed memory causes boot failure. Ensure both clips are locked.
Forgot CPU Power Connector. Common cause of boot failure. Both motherboard 24pin and CPU power must be connected.
Too Much Thermal Paste. Only a thin layer is needed—too much actually hurts cooling and can overflow causing shorts.
Front Panel Jumpers Wrong. Incorrect switch/reset jumpers cause buttons not to work. Carefully follow the manual.
Pitfall Avoidance Guide#Don’t Blindly Pursue High Specs. Hardware updates quickly—sufficient is good enough. Over-specification is wasteful.
Don’t Ignore Power Supply Quality. Poor quality power supplies can damage hardware. Power supply is one of the most worth-investing components.
Don’t Look at Single Parameters Only. CPU isn’t just about core count, GPU isn’t just about VRAM—evaluate comprehensively.
Don’t Ignore Compatibility. CPU and motherboard socket, memory generation, case dimensions all need to match.
Don’t Ignore After-sales. Choose brands and channels with complete after-sales support so you have protection when issues arise.
Conclusion#DIY PC building may seem complex, but with the core methodology mastered, you can avoid pitfalls and assemble a satisfactory system.
Key Takeaways#CPU Selection: Define purpose, buy new not old, focus on core count and single-core performance.
Graphics Card Selection: Choose NVIDIA or AMD based on needs, beware of mining cards, match power supply.
Memory Selection: Capacity first, dual channel essential, match frequency to CPU.
Storage Selection: NVMe SSD primary, 1TB minimum, focus on original NAND.
Other Components: Power supply quality is most important, motherboard power delivery must be sufficient, cooler must match CPU.
Assembly Points: Read the manual, note alignment features, secure cable connections, organize neatly.
Final Advice#DIY PC building is a learning process. Don’t be afraid of mistakes—every DIY enthusiast started as a beginner. When encountering problems, make good use of search engines and community resources. You’ll find that the problem-solving process is also part of the fun.
When you press the power button and see the screen light up, all the effort is worth it. This is not just a computer—it’s a work you built with your own hands.
Happy building!
title: PC Building Guide: From Beginner to Expert

Introduction#History is humanity’s grandest narrative, yet traditional history books often fragment their telling by region or theme, making it difficult to grasp the big picture. After reading Chinese history, European history, and American history separately, it’s hard to say what was happening around the world during the same period.
“Timelines of World History” by DK breaks this fragmentation with a simple yet powerful tool—the timeline. It horizontally unfolds human history from 3 million years ago to the 21st century, allowing you to see at a glance how different civilizations developed in parallel during the same era.
Title: Timelines of World History Publisher: DK (Dorling Kindersley) Target Readers: History enthusiasts, students, anyone seeking a global perspectiveWhat Makes This Book Unique#1. Timeline Perspective: Breaking Regional Fragmentation#The problem with traditional history books is that they are “vertically deep but horizontally narrow.” You read Chinese history and know what happened during the Tang Dynasty (618-907 AD); you read European history and know what occurred in the Middle Ages. But the Tang Dynasty and the European Middle Ages were concurrent—what connections existed between them?
This book’s core innovation is the horizontal timeline:
600 AD 700 AD 800 AD │ │ │ ├─ China: Sui-Tang ├─ Tang at peak ├─ Post-An Lushan │ prosperity │ │ Rebellion │ │ │ ├─ Europe: Early ├─ Islamic ├─ Charlemagne │ Middle Ages │ expansion │ crowned │ │ │ ├─ Middle East: ├─ Umayyad ├─ Abbasid │ Sassanid fall │ Caliphate │ Caliphate │ │ │ └─ Americas: Maya └─ Teotihuacan └─ Toltec rise city-states declineThis perspective gives you an intuitive understanding: history unfolds globally and simultaneously, with different civilizations following vastly different trajectories in the same era.
2. DK Style: Visual Presentation#DK Publishing is renowned for its “visual encyclopedia” approach, and this book continues that tradition:
Element Purpose Exquisite illustrations High-quality images of artifacts, architecture, and artworks Infographics Population changes, territorial shifts, technological developments Timeline bands Clear markers for key events at specific time points Feature pages In-depth exploration of major historical themes This is not a purely textual history book—it’s a history encyclopedia you can “see.”
3. Global Perspective: Beyond Eurocentrism#Many world history works suffer from “Eurocentrism,” overemphasizing European history. This book strives for balance:
Asia: Chinese, Indian, Japanese, and Southeast Asian histories are fully represented Africa: From ancient Egypt to the evolution of African kingdoms Americas: Maya, Aztec, and Inca civilizations Oceania: Development of Pacific island civilizations Content Structure#Overall Organization#The book is divided chronologically into major historical periods:
Chapter Time Period Core Theme Human Origins 3 million years ago - 3000 BCE From hominids to early civilizations Ancient World 3000 BCE - 500 CE Four great ancient civilizations, Greece and Rome Middle Ages 500 CE - 1500 CE Parallel development of East and West Early Modern 1500 CE - 1800 CE Age of Exploration, Renaissance, Enlightenment Modern Era 1800 CE - 1900 CE Industrial Revolution, rise of nation-states Contemporary 1900 CE - Present World Wars, globalization Content Organization Within Each Era#Each era chapter follows a “general-to-specific” structure:
Era Overview: A single-page summary of global characteristics Regional Timelines: Separate presentations for Asia, Europe, Africa, Americas, and Oceania Feature Deep-Dives: In-depth exploration of major themes For example, the “Age of Exploration” feature page details:
Breakthroughs in navigation technology Routes of major explorers Columbian Exchange between Old and New Worlds Profound impact on global格局 Reading Value#1. Building a Global Historical Perspective#After reading this book, you gain a “God’s-eye view”:
When Qin Shi Huang unified the six states, Rome was fighting Carthage for Mediterranean dominance; When the Song Dynasty enjoyed commercial prosperity, Europe was in the midst of the Crusades; When the Ming-Qing transition occurred, Europe was undergoing the Scientific Revolution.
This horizontal comparison helps you understand: history is not isolated; different civilizations’ developments influence and reflect each other.
2. Quick Historical Reference#This book serves as an “index tool” for history learning:
Want to understand an era? Turn to the corresponding chapter for a quick global overview Want to look up an event? Locate it through the timeline and understand its context Want to compare civilizations? Directly compare regional developments on the same page 3. Cultivating Historical Thinking#Timelines are not just a presentation method—they’re a way of thinking:
Causal relationships: Event A leads to Event B Parallel relationships: Similar developments in different regions during the same era Interactive relationships: Exchange and collision between civilizations Who Should Read This?#Reader Type Reading Value History beginners Build a clear historical framework, avoid fragmented knowledge Students Supplementary reading for history courses, helps understand textbook material History enthusiasts Fill knowledge gaps, gain global perspective Parents Read together with children, cultivate historical interest Travel enthusiasts Understand historical context of destinations Reading Tips#1. No Need to Read Cover to Cover#This book is suitable for browsing and reference. You can:
Start from an era that interests you Look up corresponding chapters when encountering unfamiliar historical events Use as a companion reference for other history books 2. Read with Maps#History and geography are inseparable. Suggestions:
Keep a world map handy Locate places while reading Understand how geographical environment influences historical development 3. Thematic Extension#This book offers “breadth-first” overview. If interested in specific topics, extend your reading:
Interested in Chinese history → Read “The Cambridge History of China” Interested in military history → Read “On War” or specialized military histories Interested in civilizational exchange → Read “The Silk Roads: A New History of the World” Comparison with Other History Books#Book Characteristics Suitable For DK Timelines of World History Visual, timeline-based, strong overview Building frameworks, quick reference A Global History (Stavrianos) Academic, in-depth analysis Deep learning, understanding historical patterns Sapiens (Harari) Novel perspectives, highly readable Thought-provoking, general reading Cambridge World History Academically authoritative, rich detail Professional research, in-depth reading Summary#“Timelines of World History” is an excellent history introduction and reference book. Its core value lies in:
Three Highlights#Timeline Perspective: Breaks regional fragmentation, presents global simultaneous development Visual Presentation: DK’s signature beautiful text-and-image style Global Balance: Not Eurocentric, gives equal attention to all civilizations One-Line Recommendation#If you want a book that lets you “see the whole picture of human history at a glance,” this is the best choice.
History is the best teacher, and timelines are the key to understanding it. Open this book and begin your journey through global history!
title: Timelines of World History: A Visual Journey Through Human Civilization

content:
Introduction#“What do children actually learn in kindergarten?” “Should I teach my child reading and math early?” “How do I know if my child is developing normally?” These questions concern many parents.
Early childhood education follows scientific principles. Rushing ahead with academic learning can backfire, while missing critical developmental windows may create lasting gaps. This article provides a systematic overview of developmental goals for children ages 3-6, based on authoritative standards.
Core Reference Standards: This article primarily draws from China’s Ministry of Education “Guide to the Learning and Development of Children Aged 3-6” (2012), supplemented by NAEYC Early Learning Standards, Finland’s Early Childhood Education Curriculum Framework, and other international authoritative documents.I. Overview of Authoritative Standards#China’s “Guide to the Learning and Development of Children Aged 3-6”#In 2012, China’s Ministry of Education officially released the “Guide to the Learning and Development of Children Aged 3-6” (hereinafter referred to as “The Guide”). This is China’s first systematic early childhood development guidance document. Developed over six years by nearly 100 experts and based on large-scale child development research data, it carries high scientific authority.
The Guide divides early childhood development into five domains: Health, Language, Social, Science, and Arts. Each domain contains sub-domains with clear developmental goals and educational recommendations for each age group (3-4, 4-5, and 5-6 years).
International Standards Reference#Standard/Framework Issuing Body Core Philosophy NAEYC Early Learning Standards National Association for the Education of Young Children (USA) Developmentally Appropriate Practice (DAP) Finnish ECEC Curriculum Framework Finnish National Agency for Education Play-centered, holistic development Montessori Sensitive Periods Maria Montessori Respecting children’s internal developmental rhythm UK EYFS Framework UK Department for Education Seven development areas, play-based learning While these standards differ in expression, their core philosophies are highly consistent: respect children’s developmental patterns, use play as the primary activity, and promote holistic development.
II. Health Domain: The Foundation of Physical and Mental Development#Health forms the foundation of all development. The Guide subdivides the health domain into three sub-domains: physical condition, motor development, and living habits and capabilities.
2.1 Physical Condition#Body Posture and Growth
Ages 3-6 are a period of rapid physical development. According to the “Dietary Guidelines for Chinese Residents” and WHO child growth standards, children in this age range should maintain height and weight within normal ranges:
Age Boys’ Height Range Girls’ Height Range Weight Gain Pattern 3-4 years 94.9-111.7cm 94.0-111.3cm Approximately 2kg per year 4-5 years 100.7-118.5cm 99.5-118.2cm Approximately 2kg per year 5-6 years 106.1-125.8cm 104.9-125.6cm Approximately 2-3kg per year Developmental Goals:
3-4 years: Can maintain correct standing, sitting, and walking postures with reminders 4-5 years: Can maintain correct postures with reminders, posture is generally proper 5-6 years: Regularly maintains correct standing, sitting, and walking postures naturally Emotional Management
Early childhood is a critical period for emotional development. The Guide clearly states that children should have “relatively stable emotions, rarely crying over minor matters.”
Developmental Goals:
3-4 years: Relatively stable emotions, can express emotions verbally 4-5 years: Can adapt to environmental changes relatively quickly, emotional responses are moderate 5-6 years: Can actively express emotions, beginning to learn emotional regulation 2.2 Motor Development#Motor development is divided into gross motor skills (walking, running, jumping, climbing, etc.) and fine motor skills (small muscle activities of the hands).
Gross Motor Development Goals:
Motor Type 3-4 Years 4-5 Years 5-6 Years Walk-run alternation Can alternate walking and running for 150m Can alternate walking and running for 200m Can alternate walking and running for 250m Single-leg hopping Can hop 2-3 steps on one leg Can hop 5-8 steps on one leg Can hop 8-10 steps on one leg Throwing Can throw approximately 2 meters Can throw 3-4 meters Can throw 4-5 meters Fine Motor Development Goals:
3-4 years: Can cut along straight lines with scissors, edges basically aligned; can fold paper edge-to-edge and corner-to-corner 4-5 years: Can cut out simple straight-line shapes along outlines; can use chopsticks for eating 5-6 years: Can cut out simple curved shapes along outlines; can use simple tools and materials to make toys 2.3 Living Habits and Capabilities#Good living habits form the foundation of lifelong health.
Key Cultivation Areas:
Regular Schedule: Develop the habit of early to bed, early to rise; ensure 11-13 hours of sleep daily (including nap) Eating Habits: Not picky or partial with food, can eat independently Hygiene Habits: Wash hands before meals and after using the toilet, brush teeth morning and evening, bathe and change clothes regularly Safety Awareness: Don’t go with strangers, don’t play with dangerous items, know emergency contact methods Developmental Goals:
3-4 years: Goes to bed and gets up on time with reminders, can dress and undress simple clothing 4-5 years: Goes to bed and gets up on time daily, can dress and undress, fasten buttons 5-6 years: Has developed regular schedule habits, can organize own belongings, knows basic safety knowledge III. Language Domain: Tools for Communication and Thinking#Language is both a tool for thinking and a foundation for social interaction. The Guide divides the language domain into two sub-domains: listening and speaking, and reading and writing preparation.
3.1 Listening and Speaking#Listening Development Goals:
Age Listening Goals 3-4 years Pays attention and responds when spoken to; understands daily conversations 4-5 years Consciously listens to information related to oneself in groups; understands relatively complex sentences expressing cause-effect and hypothesis in context 5-6 years Pays attention when teachers or others speak in groups; asks questions proactively when not understanding Speaking Development Goals:
3-4 years: Willing to speak in front of familiar people, can greet others confidently; can recite nursery rhymes or retell short stories clearly 4-5 years: Willing to discuss problems with others, dares to speak in front of crowds; can relatively completely narrate own experiences 5-6 years: Speaks confidently in front of groups with clear voice; can narrate an event in an orderly, coherent, and clear manner Note for Parents: Language development varies individually; girls typically develop slightly faster than boys. If a child cannot speak simple sentences by age 4, consulting a professional is recommended.
3.2 Reading and Writing Preparation#Important Note: “Write preparation” in early childhood is not teaching writing, but cultivating the basic capabilities needed for writing.
Reading Interest Development Goals:
3-4 years: Enjoys listening to stories and looking at picture books, actively requests adults to tell stories and read books 4-5 years: Can repeatedly look at favorite books, enjoys telling heard stories to others 5-6 years: Focuses on reading books, can imagine and create based on story plots or picture content Writing Preparation Capabilities:
Capability Dimension Specific Content Hand strength Tearing paper, kneading clay, stringing beads to exercise small hand muscles Hand-eye coordination Drawing, coloring, tracing to develop coordination Spatial cognition Distinguishing up-down, left-right, inside-outside concepts Shape cognition Recognizing basic shapes, understanding relationships between shapes Myth Clarification: Teaching children to write early is not only unnecessary but may cause incorrect pencil grip and aversion to writing. First grade is the appropriate stage for formal writing instruction.
IV. Social Domain: Interpersonal Relationships and Social Adaptation#Social development determines children’s future interpersonal relationships and social adaptation capabilities. The Guide divides the social domain into two sub-domains: interpersonal relationships and social adaptation.
4.1 Interpersonal Relationships#Social Attitude Development Goals:
Age Goal Description 3-4 years Willing to play games with other children; willing to participate in activities with familiar elders 4-5 years Enjoys playing games with other children, has regular playmates; willing to converse with elders 5-6 years Has own good friends, willing to share; willing to seek advice from others when facing problems Social Skills Development Points:
Sharing and Cooperation: Learning to take turns, wait, share toys and materials Conflict Resolution: Can resolve conflicts with peers with adult help Empathy: Can notice others’ emotions and show care and consideration Rule Awareness: Understands and follows game rules and group rules Developmental Goals:
3-4 years: Doesn’t fight over or monopolize toys with adult guidance; can follow adult mediation during peer conflicts 4-5 years: Can take turns sharing things everyone likes; knows to thank for received gifts 5-6 years: Can find ways to attract peers to play together; can divide work and cooperate with peers during activities 4.2 Social Adaptation#Self-Cognition Development:
3-4 years: Can state own name, age, gender; knows own belongings 4-5 years: Can state the name of own home location; knows the name of own kindergarten 5-6 years: Knows own ethnic group, knows China is a multi-ethnic family Social Rules and Sense of Belonging:
Development Dimension 3-4 Years 4-5 Years 5-6 Years Rule awareness Can follow game and public place rules with reminders Feels the meaning of rules and can basically follow them Understands the meaning of rules, can negotiate game rules with peers Sense of belonging Likes own kindergarten and class Likes own group Cares about the collective, has preliminary collective honor V. Science Domain: Inquiry Spirit and Mathematical Thinking#The science domain cultivates children’s inquiry spirit and logical thinking, divided into two sub-domains: scientific inquiry and mathematical cognition.
5.1 Scientific Inquiry#Cultivating Inquiry Interest:
Children are naturally “little scientists,” curious about the world around them. Education’s task is to protect and guide this curiosity.
Developmental Goals:
3-4 years: Enjoys contact with nature, interested in many surrounding things and phenomena; frequently asks various questions 4-5 years: Enjoys contact with new things, frequently asks questions related to new things; often uses hands and brain to explore objects and materials 5-6 years: Always gets to the bottom of interesting questions; frequently uses hands and brain to find answers to questions Inquiry Capability Development:
Capability Dimension Specific Performance Observation ability Can use multiple senses to perceive object characteristics, notice obvious changes Comparison and classification Can classify objects based on obvious characteristics Recording and expression Can record observations using drawings, symbols, etc. Experimental exploration Can verify hypotheses through simple experiments Daily Science Activity Suggestions:
Nature Observation: Observe plant growth, seasonal changes, insect activities, etc. Simple Experiments: Sink-float experiments, magnet games, shadow changes, etc. Planting and Raising: Plant simple plants, raise small animals Technology Experience: Visit science museums, use simple tools 5.2 Mathematical Cognition#Important Note: Early childhood mathematics is not doing arithmetic problems, but cultivating mathematical thinking and interest in mathematics.
Mathematical Ability Development Goals:
Content Dimension 3-4 Years 4-5 Years 5-6 Years Number concept Can count up to 5 objects with one-to-one correspondence Can count up to 10 objects, understands number-quantity relationship Can perform addition and subtraction within 10, understands the meaning of addition and subtraction Shape and space Can notice obvious shape characteristics of objects Can perceive basic spatial positions and directions Can use spatial words like up-down, front-back, inside-outside, middle, beside Comparison and measurement Can compare object size, length Can compare object height, thickness Can measure objects using natural materials, understands the meaning of measurement Patterns Can classify objects by one characteristic Can classify objects by two characteristics Can discover simple arrangement patterns Mathematics in Daily Life:
Mathematics learning should be integrated into daily life:
Count steps when going up and down stairs Compare quantities when distributing fruit Sort and organize when tidying toys Recognize time concepts using calendars Myth Clarification: Learning elementary mathematics content early (such as two-digit addition and subtraction) does more harm than good for young children. What children need is concrete, operable mathematical experiences, not abstract symbolic operations.
VI. Arts Domain: Perceiving and Expressing Beauty#The arts domain cultivates children’s aesthetic abilities and creativity, divided into two sub-domains: perception and appreciation, and expression and creation.
6.1 Perception and Appreciation#Aesthetic Perception Development Goals:
3-4 years: Enjoys listening to music or watching dance, theater, and other performances; enjoys viewing paintings, clay sculptures, or other art forms 4-5 years: Enjoys listening to various pleasant sounds, perceives changes in pitch, duration, and intensity of sounds; can attentively watch favorite performances or artworks 5-6 years: Enjoys appreciating various art forms and works; willing to share and discuss favorite artworks with others Aesthetic Experience Cultivation:
Experience Type Specific Content Music appreciation Experience different styles of music, feel rhythm and melody beauty Visual arts appreciation Appreciate paintings, sculptures, architecture, and other artworks Natural beauty Feel the beauty of natural scenery and seasonal changes Beauty in life Discover beautiful things in daily life 6.2 Expression and Creation#Artistic Expression Development Goals:
Age Music Expression Visual Arts Expression 3-4 years Can imitate and sing short songs; can imitate simple rhythmic movements Enjoys drawing, pasting, and sticking activities 4-5 years Can sing with natural, moderate volume voice; can do simple rhythmic movements with music Can use painting, handicrafts, and other forms to express imagination 5-6 years Can sing with basically accurate rhythm and pitch; can express musical emotions through rhythm or simple dance movements Can use multiple tools, materials, or different expression techniques to express feelings and imagination Principles for Cultivating Creativity:
Respect Originality: Don’t judge children’s work by adult standards; “does it look like it” is not the evaluation criterion Provide Materials: Provide rich materials and tools for free exploration Encourage Expression: Encourage children to express thoughts and feelings through art forms Avoid Imposition: Don’t teach fixed painting techniques or music performance patterns Myth Clarification: The core of early childhood art education is not skill training, but cultivating aesthetic ability and creativity. Premature professional training may stifle children’s creativity and artistic interest.
VII. Age-Specific Developmental Priorities#Based on the developmental goals across five domains, each age group has different developmental priorities:
7.1 Junior Class (3-4 Years): Adaptation and Habits#Core Tasks: Adapt to kindergarten life, establish basic living habits
Domain Development Priorities Health Adapt to collective life, develop basic habits like hand-washing, toileting Language Willing to speak, can understand daily instructions Social Willing to attend kindergarten, can get along peacefully with peers Science Interested in surrounding things, can make simple observations Arts Enjoys singing, drawing, willing to participate in art activities 7.2 Middle Class (4-5 Years): Social Interaction and Exploration#Core Tasks: Develop social skills, cultivate inquiry interest
Domain Development Priorities Health More coordinated movements, can dress and undress independently Language Can express clearly, enjoys listening to stories and reading books Social Has good friends, can follow basic rules Science Enjoys asking why, can make simple comparisons and classifications Arts Can express own ideas through art forms 7.3 Senior Class (5-6 Years): Independence and Preparation#Core Tasks: Cultivate independence, prepare for school entry
Domain Development Priorities Health Has good living habits, mature motor development Language Can express coherently, has reading interest and habits Social Has rule awareness, can cooperate to solve problems Science Has inquiry spirit, has preliminary mathematical thinking Arts Can creatively express beauty, has aesthetic judgment ability School Readiness Points:
School preparation is not learning elementary curriculum in advance, but cultivating the following capabilities:
Self-care: Can independently complete basic life tasks Task awareness: Can remember and complete assigned tasks Rule awareness: Understands and follows collective rules Focus ability: Can concentrate on completing tasks Social skills: Can get along friendly with peers Language expression: Can clearly express own thoughts and needs VIII. Recommendations for Parents#8.1 Understanding Early Childhood Education Correctly#Three “Don’ts”:
Don’t rush development: Respect children’s developmental patterns, don’t teach elementary content early Don’t overprotect: Give children opportunities to try and solve problems themselves Don’t compare horizontally: Each child develops at their own pace; avoid “other people’s children” comparisons Three “Dos”:
Do accompany and observe: Understand your child’s interests and developmental level Do create environment: Provide rich materials and experience opportunities Do wait patiently: Give children enough time to grow 8.2 Supporting Education at Home#Educational Opportunities in Daily Life:
Scenario Cultivatable Capabilities Getting up and dressing Self-care ability, time concept Eating meals Eating habits, number concept (distributing utensils) Going out Observation ability, safety awareness, social skills Bedtime stories Language ability, reading interest Doing chores Sense of responsibility, hands-on ability 8.3 Communication with Kindergarten#Understand the kindergarten’s educational philosophy and curriculum Participate in parent meetings, open days, and other activities Communicate with teachers about your child’s situation in a timely manner Cooperate with the kindergarten in family education Conclusion#Ages 3-6 are a critical period in life, but “critical” does not mean rushing ahead. The essence of early childhood education is laying the foundation for lifelong development, not learning knowledge and skills in advance.
Understanding children’s developmental patterns, respecting children’s developmental rhythms, and creating rich experience opportunities in daily life—this is the core of scientific parenting.
I hope this article helps parents develop a scientific understanding of early childhood development and provide appropriate support and guidance during this critical period of their children’s growth.
References and Resources#Ministry of Education of the People’s Republic of China. Guide to the Learning and Development of Children Aged 3-6[M]. Capital Normal University Press, 2012. China National Society of Early Childhood Education. Interpretation of the “Guide to the Learning and Development of Children Aged 3-6”[M]. People’s Education Press, 2013. NAEYC. Developmentally Appropriate Practice in Early Childhood Programs Serving Children from Birth Through Age 8 (4th Edition)[M]. 2022. Finnish National Agency for Education. National Core Curriculum for Early Childhood Education and Care 2022. Li Jimei, Feng Xiaoxia. Interpretation of the “Guide to the Learning and Development of Children Aged 3-6”[M]. People’s Education Press, 2013. Hua Aihua. Kindergarten Curriculum Gamification: What is “Gamification”[J]. Studies in Preschool Education, 2016. Yu Yongping. Kindergarten-Elementary Transition: Dilemmas and Solutions[J]. People’s Education, 2021. Further Reading:
Full Text of China’s “Guide to the Learning and Development of Children Aged 3-6” NAEYC Official Website - Family Resources
title: Early Childhood Development Guide: Understanding What Children Learn in Kindergarten (Ages 3-6)
